references.title: Permanent standard time is the optimal choice for health and safety: an American Academy of Sleep Medicine position statement
references.abstract: The American Academy of Sleep Medicine advocates for permanent standard time, aligning with human circadian rhythms and reducing health risks associated with daylight saving time.

references.title: New position statement supports permanent standard time
references.abstract: The American Academy of Sleep Medicine supports replacing daylight saving time with permanent standard time to better align with human circadian biology.

references.title: SRS position statement supports adoption of permanent standard time
references.abstract: The Sleep Research Society advocates for eliminating daylight saving time in favor of permanent standard time to align with human circadian rhythms.

references.title: Stanford scientists reveal simple shift that could prevent strokes and obesity nationwide
references.abstract: Stanford researchers found that permanent standard time could reduce health risks associated with biannual clock changes, including strokes and obesity.

references.title: Daylight saving time tied to higher rates of stroke and obesity
references.abstract: A Stanford Medicine study links daylight saving time to increased rates of stroke and obesity, advocating for permanent standard time.

claim_text: AI-generated explicit content involving minors needs to be treated with the seriousness it deserves.
query:
Task: Provide up to 25 scientific references relevant to the claim.
Context: California legislative press release analysis.
Claim (senator-ochoa-bogh-advocates-californians-urging-action-governor-key-legislative-issues.txt / #3): AI-generated explicit content involving minors needs to be treated with the seriousness it deserves.
Constraints:
- Prioritize peer-reviewed sources.
- Include DOI when available.
- Keep abstracts to 1-2 sentence summaries if exact abstracts not available.
- Prefer recency for fast-moving topics (e.g., deepfakes, LLMs), else include seminal works.
- provider = discovery source (e.g., 'arXiv' if link is on arxiv.org; 'publisher' for publisher site);
- venue = actual journal or conference (use 'arXiv' only if there is no published venue).
- Normalize venue names to standard short forms (e.g., NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, CVPR, ICCV, ECCV, AAAI, IJCAI, ACL, EMNLP, NAACL).
Output: JSON object {"references": [{provider,id,doi,title,abstract,year,venue,authors,url,is_open_access}...]}.

references.title: Deepfakes and Digitally Altered Imagery Abuse: A Cross-Country Exploration of an Emerging form of Image-Based Sexual Abuse
references.abstract: Multi-country criminology study documenting the prevalence, harms, and legal/policing challenges of deepfake and digitally altered sexual imagery as a form of image-based sexual abuse, with implications for young people and minors.

references.title: Prevalence of Online Sexual Offenses Against Children in the US
references.abstract: National survey estimates show substantial lifetime exposure of minors to online sexual offenses and image-based sexual abuse, underscoring the scope and seriousness of technology‑facilitated harms to children.

references.title: Forensic psychological procedures in cases of technology‑facilitated sexual abuse among adolescents: A scoping review
references.abstract: Scoping review of forensic assessment procedures for adolescent victims of technology‑facilitated sexual abuse, highlighting evidence needs and risks of harm when digital evidence is involved.

references.title: The Characteristics of Virtual Child Sexual Abuse Material Offenders and the Harms of Offending: A Qualitative Content Analysis of Print Media
references.abstract: Analyzes media‑reported cases involving virtual/AI‑like CSAM to characterize offenders and document harms, reinforcing that synthetic CSAM is treated as serious sexual offending.

references.title: A deep learning framework for finding illicit images/videos of children
references.abstract: Presents a practical CSAM triage framework combining age estimation and nudity detection to help identify child sexual exploitation content, illustrating technical feasibility and constraints.

references.title: Seeing without Looking: Analysis Pipeline for Child Sexual Abuse Datasets
references.abstract: Introduces a privacy‑preserving analysis pipeline for CSAM datasets that surfaces aggregate signals without exposing images, supporting safer research and tool development.

references.title: Anogenital Findings in Confirmed Victims of Child Sexual Abuse Materials
references.abstract: Retrospective study of children documented in CSAM shows most had normal exams and limited disclosure, emphasizing that absence of physical findings does not negate serious abuse.

references.title: Metadata‑Based Detection of Child Sexual Abuse Material
references.abstract: Proposes and evaluates metadata‑only machine learning to detect CSAM at scale without accessing illegal imagery, achieving high accuracy and robustness to adversarial obfuscation.
references.venue: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing

references.title: Deepfake detection with and without content warnings
references.abstract: Experimental evidence shows people struggle to spot high‑quality deepfakes even when warned, indicating human discernment alone cannot reliably identify synthetic sexual imagery.

references.title: Threat of deepfakes to the criminal justice system: a systematic review
references.abstract: Systematic review mapping deepfake‑related threats—including sexual abuse content—to evidence integrity and enforcement, calling for stronger detection and legal responses.

references.title: Moderating online child sexual abuse material (CSAM): Does self‑regulation work, or is greater state regulation needed?
references.abstract: Reviews platform moderation of CSAM and argues that self‑regulation is insufficient, supporting stronger statutory measures given the scale and harms of CSAM online.

references.title: Designed to abuse? Deepfakes and the non‑consensual diffusion of intimate images
references.abstract: Philosophical and normative analysis of deepfakes as tools for non‑consensual intimate imagery, articulating unique victim harms and justifying serious regulatory responses.

references.title: Contrastive Language‑Vision AI Models Pretrained on Web‑Scraped Multimodal Data Exhibit Sexual Objectification Bias
references.abstract: FAccT study shows text‑to‑image systems can generate sexualized depictions when prompted with ages (e.g., ‘[age]‑year‑old girl’), evidencing risks that models sexualize minors and require strict safeguards.

references.title: Non‑Consensual Synthetic Intimate Imagery: Prevalence, Attitudes, and Knowledge in 10 Countries
references.abstract: Large cross‑national survey estimates victimization and perpetration of deepfake pornography, finding perceived harms and gaps in legislation and platform enforcement.

references.title: SneakyPrompt: Jailbreaking Text‑to‑Image Generative Models
references.abstract: Demonstrates automated prompt‑based jailbreaks that bypass safety filters to produce NSFW imagery, highlighting the ease of generating prohibited sexual content despite safeguards.

references.title: EvilPromptFuzzer: generating inappropriate content based on text‑to‑image models
references.abstract: Presents a fuzz‑testing framework that systematically crafts prompts to elicit unsafe content from T2I systems and shows commercial moderation often fails to block generated inappropriate images.

references.title: SafeGen: Mitigating Sexually Explicit Content Generation in Text‑to‑Image Models
references.abstract: Introduces an internal representation editing approach that suppresses explicit sexual content generation across prompts, improving resistance to adversarial jailbreaks.

references.title: PromptGuard: Soft Prompt‑Guided Unsafe Content Moderation for Text‑to‑Image Models
references.abstract: Proposes a universal safety soft‑prompt to moderate NSFW generations without altering inference speed, outperforming prior defenses on multiple datasets.

references.title: SoK: Facial Deepfake Detectors
references.abstract: Systematizes state‑of‑the‑art facial deepfake detectors and benchmarks generalization under varied attack scenarios, noting limitations relevant to policing synthetic sexual content.

references.title: The Mental Health and Social Implications of Nonconsensual Sharing of Intimate Images on Youth: A Systematic Review
references.abstract: Synthesizes evidence that youth victims of image‑based sexual abuse experience depression, anxiety, suicidal ideation, bullying, and school disruption—harms likely to also arise from AI‑generated sexual imagery involving minors.

references.title: Child Sexual Abuse Material facilitated by Artificial Intelligence
references.abstract: Qualitative review of how AI can facilitate production and distribution of CSAM and the current legislative gaps, arguing for stronger, harmonized responses.

references.title: Artificial intelligence and child sexual abuse: A rapid evidence assessment
references.abstract: Peer‑reviewed rapid evidence assessment (2010–2024) of AI’s role in preventing/disrupting CSA and in enabling CSA offending, identifying research gaps and policy needs.
references.venue: Australian Institute of Criminology – Trends & Issues in Crime and Criminal Justice

claim_text: As AI technology advances, it is crucial that laws evolve to protect children from new forms of exploitation.
query:
Task: Provide up to 25 scientific references relevant to the claim.
Context: California legislative press release analysis.
Claim (senator-ochoa-bogh-advocates-californians-urging-action-governor-key-legislative-issues.txt / #4): As AI technology advances, it is crucial that laws evolve to protect children from new forms of exploitation.
Constraints:
- Prioritize peer-reviewed sources.
- Include DOI when available.
- Keep abstracts to 1-2 sentence summaries if exact abstracts not available.
- Prefer recency for fast-moving topics (e.g., deepfakes, LLMs), else include seminal works.
- provider = discovery source (e.g., 'arXiv' if link is on arxiv.org; 'publisher' for publisher site);
- venue = actual journal or conference (use 'arXiv' only if there is no published venue).
- Normalize venue names to standard short forms (e.g., NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, CVPR, ICCV, ECCV, AAAI, IJCAI, ACL, EMNLP, NAACL).
Output: JSON object {"references": [{provider,id,doi,title,abstract,year,venue,authors,url,is_open_access}...]}.

references.title: Prevalence of Online Sexual Offenses Against Children in the US
references.abstract: A nationally representative survey of U.S. young adults reports lifetime prevalence of various forms of online child sexual abuse, including grooming and sextortion, underscoring the scope of technology-facilitated exploitation and the need for prevention and policy responses.

references.title: Which dynamics make online child sexual abuse and cyberstalking more emotionally impactful: Perpetrator identity and images?
references.abstract: Using retrospective reports from U.S. young adults, the study finds higher emotional harm when sexual images are misused (e.g., non‑consensual sharing or sextortion) and highlights that peers and acquaintances frequently perpetrate these harms.

references.title: Learning to Break Deep Perceptual Hashing: The Use Case NeuralHash
references.abstract: This paper demonstrates practical evasion and collision attacks against a deep perceptual hashing system proposed for CSAM detection, raising reliability and safety concerns for client‑side scanning approaches.

references.title: Bugs in our pockets: the risks of client-side scanning
references.abstract: Analyzes client‑side scanning proposals for detecting CSAM and other harms, concluding they introduce serious security, privacy, and governance risks that are difficult to mitigate at scale.

references.title: Online grooming detection: A comprehensive survey of child exploitation in chat logs
references.abstract: A systematic survey of datasets and machine learning methods for detecting online grooming in chat conversations, outlining challenges and future directions for more reliable and ethical detection.

references.title: Metadata-Based Detection of Child Sexual Abuse Material
references.abstract: Proposes a CSAM detection framework trained on file‑path metadata from real investigations, demonstrating high accuracy while avoiding legal and ethical constraints of using contraband imagery in training.
references.venue: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing

references.title: Mapping the Interdisciplinary Research on Non-consensual Pornography: Technical and Quantitative Perspectives
references.abstract: A systematic mapping study synthesizing technical and quantitative research on non‑consensual pornography (including deepfakes), identifying gaps and priorities for tool development, moderation, and regulation.

references.title: Threat of deepfakes to the criminal justice system: a systematic review
references.abstract: Reviews how deepfakes undermine evidence integrity and facilitate crimes including sexual abuse imagery, emphasizing the need for legal safeguards and improved detection to maintain justice system trust.

references.title: Designed to abuse? Deepfakes and the non-consensual diffusion of intimate images
references.abstract: Philosophical analysis argues that deepfakes enable distinctive epistemic and moral harms in image‑based abuse, motivating stronger governance and accountability for synthetic media tools.

references.title: Rising Threats of AI-Driven Child Sexual Abuse Material
references.abstract: A pediatrics perspective highlighting the rapid growth of AI‑generated and AI‑manipulated CSAM, associated risks to youth, and the need for coordinated clinical, technological, and policy responses.

references.title: The Legal Challenges of Realistic and AI-Driven Child Sexual Abuse Material: Regulatory and Enforcement Perspectives in Europe
references.abstract: Analyzes European legal frameworks facing AI‑generated CSAM, discussing enforcement challenges and proposing regulatory approaches to address realistic synthetic depictions of minors.

references.title: The ‘new voyeurism’: criminalizing the creation of ‘deepfake porn’
references.abstract: Legal analysis argues for criminalizing the creation of sexually explicit deepfakes, detailing harms—including to women and girls—and recommending urgent legislative reform alongside broader measures.

references.title: A Survey of Perceptual Hashing for Multimedia
references.abstract: Comprehensive survey of perceptual hashing techniques for images and video, covering classical and learning‑based schemes and their security/robustness—core to large‑scale detection of illegal content.
references.venue: ACM Transactions on Multimedia Computing, Communications, and Applications

references.title: Robustness of Practical Perceptual Hashing Algorithms to Hash-Evasion and Hash-Inversion Attacks
references.abstract: Evaluates the security of widely used perceptual hashing algorithms (PhotoDNA, PDQ, NeuralHash), finding notable robustness under certain conditions and proposing defenses to improve security.

references.title: Non-Consensual Synthetic Intimate Imagery: Prevalence, Attitudes, and Knowledge in 10 Countries
references.abstract: A 10‑country survey (n>16,000) measures victimization and perpetration of deepfake pornography, finding nontrivial prevalence and gaps in legal deterrence—implications for platform policy and law.

references.title: Comparing Machine Learning Models with a Focus on Tone in Grooming Chat Logs
references.abstract: Compares traditional ML models and a compact LLM for detecting grooming and examines how predator conversational tone affects detection, informing practical moderation strategies.

references.title: When Is Online Sexual Solicitation of a Minor Considered Sexual Abuse? Recommendations for Victim Prevalence Surveys
references.abstract: Offers measurement guidance to better capture online sexual solicitation and abuse of minors in prevalence surveys, improving comparability for policy and prevention.

references.title: Economic Evaluation of Prevention Interventions for Child Sexual Exploitation or Child Sexual Abuse: A Systematic Review
references.abstract: Synthesizes economic evaluations of CSA/CSE prevention programs, informing policymakers on cost‑effectiveness considerations when designing protections for children.

references.title: Cybergrooming Victimization Among Young People: A Systematic Review of Prevalence Rates, Risk Factors, and Outcomes
references.abstract: Systematic review indicates at least one in ten youths experience cybergrooming, identifies associated risk factors, and underscores the need for targeted prevention and support.

references.title: Characterizing the MrDeepFakes Sexual Deepfake Marketplace
references.abstract: Empirical study of a large sexual deepfake marketplace documents supply chains, enforcement gaps, and attacker practices, informing governance and platform policy responses.

references.title: Detecting Online Grooming By Simple Contrastive Chat Embeddings
references.abstract: Introduces a contrastive‑learning approach for grooming detection in chats, showing competitive performance with simpler architectures suitable for deployment.
references.venue: Proceedings of the ACM International Workshop on Security and Privacy Analytics

references.title: The rise of AI and deepfake child abuse
references.abstract: Editorial summarizing recent evidence and watchdog reports on AI‑generated child sexual abuse imagery and calling for multi‑level responses across law, platforms, and practice.
references.venue: Journal of Family and Child Health

references.title: Senator Suzette Valladares’ Bill to Increase Penalties for Crimes During State of Emergency Signed Into Law
references.abstract: Senator Suzette Martinez Valladares announced that her legislation, Senate Bill 571, has been signed into law by the Governor, increasing criminal penalties for offenses committed during a declared state of emergency, including crimes such as impersonating first responders and looting.

references.title: Public Safety Committee Approves Bill to Increase Penalties for Crimes During State of Emergency
references.abstract: The Senate Public Safety Committee approved Senate Bill 571, authored by Senator Suzette Valladares, which seeks to increase California’s criminal penalties for perpetrators seeking to take advantage of victims during a state of emergency, including making looting a felony and enhancing penalties for impersonating first responders.

references.title: Governor Newsom signs bipartisan legislation to boost ongoing Los Angeles rebuilding efforts, strengthen future disaster response and recovery
references.abstract: Governor Newsom signed bipartisan legislation, including SB 571, which seeks to thwart those who impersonate first responders to gain access to evacuation zones, particularly to loot, by creating a new crime of fraudulently impersonating a first responder in an area subject to an evacuation order.

references.title: Sen Valladares’ Bill to Increase Penalties for Crimes During State of Emergency Gets Senate Approval
references.abstract: Senator Suzette Martinez Valladares announced that her bill (SB 571) to increase penalties for looting and impersonating law enforcement during a state of emergency has passed out of the Senate with unanimous bipartisan support, aiming to protect victims from opportunistic criminals during such times.

references.title: Bill Text: CA SB571 | 2025-2026 | Regular Session | Amended | LegiScan
references.abstract: The text of California Senate Bill 571, which provides for increased penalties for impersonating a first responder in an area subject to an evacuation order and considers the fact that a person convicted of looting committed the offense while impersonating emergency personnel as a factor in aggravation at sentencing.

references.title: SB 571
references.abstract: California Senate Bill 571, as amended on May 23, 2025, provides for increased penalties for impersonating a first responder in an area subject to an evacuation order and considers the fact that a person convicted of looting committed the offense while impersonating emergency personnel as a factor in aggravation at sentencing.

references.title: SB 265 - Looting During a State or Local Emergency
references.abstract: Senate Bill 265 increases penalties for burglaries committed during a wildfire or other emergencies, classifying them as felonies, to send a strong message that it is unacceptable to steal and loot from suffering individuals in vulnerable communities.

references.title: California lawmakers want tougher penalties for looting and first responder shams
references.abstract: A coalition of state lawmakers introduced legislation to strengthen penalties for looting and impersonating emergency personnel during wildfires and other emergencies, aiming to close loopholes in existing law that could hinder prosecution.

references.title: Governor Newsom deploys California National Guard to Los Angeles fires: ‘Looting will not be tolerated’
references.abstract: Governor Gavin Newsom announced the deployment of the California National Guard to support law enforcement efforts in Southern California, emphasizing that looting will not be tolerated during the ongoing wildfires.

references.title: Senate Committee Unanimously Approves Measure by Sen Valladares to Expand Healthcare Access for Cancer Patients
references.abstract: On April 7, 2025, the Senate Business, Professions & Economic Development Committee unanimously approved Senate Bill 508, introduced by Senator Suzette Valladares, aiming to provide cancer patients with greater flexibility and access to their established specialized care providers through telehealth services.

references.title: Senator Valladares’ Bill to Expand Health Access for Cancer Patients Gets Senate Approval
references.abstract: On May 29, 2025, Senate Bill 508, authored by Senator Suzette Valladares, passed unanimously out of the Senate, aiming to expand the continuum of care for cancer patients by allowing them to consult with established out-of-state physicians through telehealth platforms.

references.title: Senator Suzette Valladares Introduces Bill to Expand Health Access for Cancer Patients
references.abstract: On March 6, 2025, Senator Suzette Valladares introduced Senate Bill 508, aiming to expand access to life-saving cancer care by allowing California cancer patients to consult with out-of-state physicians through telehealth platforms.

references.title: Bill to Expand Telehealth Options for California Cancer Patients Passes Senate Committee
references.abstract: On April 7, 2025, Senate Bill 508, authored by Senator Suzette Valladares, passed the Senate Business, Professions & Economic Development Committee unanimously, aiming to expand health access for cancer patients by allowing them to consult with out-of-state doctors remotely.

references.title: ATA ACTION SUPPORT OF SB 508
references.abstract: On June 19, 2025, ATA Action expressed support for Senate Bill 508, which relates to cross-state licensure for providers treating patients diagnosed with cancer, allowing licensed out-of-state providers to deliver telehealth services to California patients.

references.title: Agenda Item 7K
references.abstract: On May 15, 2025, the Medical Board of California discussed Senate Bill 508, which would expand telehealth access for cancer patients by allowing them to consult with out-of-state physicians without obtaining a California medical license.

references.title: Agenda Item 10K
references.abstract: On August 21, 2025, the Medical Board of California discussed Senate Bill 508, which would expand the exemption allowing certain out-of-state physicians to treat California patients via telehealth without obtaining a California medical license.

references.title: CA SB508 - California 2025-2026 Regular Session
references.abstract: An overview of Senate Bill 508, introduced by Senator Valladares, which allows licensed out-of-state physicians to provide telehealth services to California patients diagnosed with any stage of cancer, expanding access to specialized care across state lines.

references.title: California’s Film & TV Tax Credit Generates $2.6 Billion in Annual Production Spending, Expands Workforce Training/Diversity and Addresses COVID Compliance Costs, Report Shows
references.abstract: The California Film Commission's report highlights that the Film & TV Tax Credit Program generated $2.6 billion in production spending during its first fiscal year, supporting 8,000 crew members and 5,000 cast members, and promoting workforce training and diversity initiatives.

references.title: Governor Newsom announces tax credit awards for new job-creating films shooting in California and generating $1.4 billion for the state’s economy
references.abstract: Governor Gavin Newsom announced that 52 film projects received tax credits under California’s Film and Television Tax Credit Program, projected to inject over $1.4 billion into the state’s economy and employ an estimated 8,900 cast and crew members, supporting local businesses and communities.

references.title: DGA Celebrates Passage of AB 1138, The California Film and Television Jobs Program
references.abstract: The Directors Guild of America celebrates the passage of AB 1138, which more than doubles the funding for the California Film and Television Jobs Program, aiming to retain and grow jobs, support small businesses, and reestablish California's competitive edge in the global entertainment market.

references.title: Landmark Film and TV Jobs Legislation Passes Legislature, Heads to Governor’s Desk
references.abstract: AB 1138, the California Film & Television Jobs Act, has passed both the Assembly and Senate with bipartisan support and is now headed to Governor Gavin Newsom's desk for signature, aiming to modernize and expand California's Film and Television Tax Credit Program to retain and grow jobs and support small businesses.

references.title: California Film & Television Jobs Act Advances Out of Key Policy Committee
references.abstract: AB 1138, the California Film & Television Jobs Act, has advanced out of the Assembly Committee on Arts, Entertainment, Sports, & Tourism, aiming to strengthen and modernize the California Film & Television Tax Credit to retain and expand entertainment industry jobs and reinforce California's status as a global leader in creativity and innovation.

references.title: Key Elements of Bills to Modernize California Film & TV Tax Credit Unveiled
references.abstract: AB 1138 and SB 630, introduced by Assemblymember Rick Chavez Zbur and Senator Ben Allen, aim to strengthen and modernize the California Film & TV Tax Credit, keeping productions and jobs in California, with key provisions to make the program more competitive with other states and countries.

references.title: California Doubles Down on Hollywood: $750M in Tax Credits Approved for Film and TV Industry
references.abstract: California has approved $750 million in tax credits for the film and TV industry, with the base credit rising from 20% to 35%, and the potential to reach 40% for productions filmed outside Los Angeles County, aiming to keep productions and jobs in California.

references.title: Incentive Alert: California's Film & TV Tax Credit Program Gets a Major Boost, Effective July 2025
references.abstract: California's Film & TV Tax Credit Program is set to receive a major boost, effective July 2025, with increased credit rates and expanded eligibility to cover projects such as animated films and series, large-scale competition shows, and TV shows with episodes of less than 30 minutes, aiming to keep productions and jobs in California.

references.title: California's Film Tax Incentive Expands to $750M in 2025
references.abstract: California's Film Tax Incentive has expanded to $750 million in 2025, with qualified productions now eligible for a 35-40% refundable incentive, aiming to keep productions and jobs in California and compete with other states and countries offering tax incentives.

references.title: SB 405: Restoring Local Control for Voter Identification Decisions
references.abstract: California Senate Bill 405, introduced by Senator Steven Choi, aims to repeal existing prohibitions against local governments requiring voter identification, thereby restoring local control over voter ID decisions.

references.title: California-2025-SB405-Introduced
references.abstract: Text of California Senate Bill 405, introduced on February 14, 2025, which seeks to repeal Section 10005 of the Elections Code, allowing local governments to implement voter ID requirements.

references.title: Choi: Give the Voters What They Want!
references.abstract: Senator Steven Choi discusses the introduction of Senate Bill 405, emphasizing the majority of Californians' support for voter ID requirements and the bill's intent to restore local control over such decisions.

references.title: California voter ID bill voted down at the state Capitol
references.abstract: Coverage of the failure of Assembly Bill 25, which aimed to establish voter ID laws in California, highlighting the legislative challenges and partisan divisions surrounding voter ID requirements.

references.title: New California Law Prohibits Localities From Imposing Their Own Voter ID Requirements
references.abstract: Analysis of Senate Bill 1174, signed into law in 2024, which prohibits local governments in California from requiring voter identification, and its implications for local control over election procedures.

references.title: Drug & Alcohol Overdose Hospitalization & Death in Orange County
references.abstract: This report provides a summary of the leading causes of substance-related overdoses in Orange County, highlighting a dramatic rise from 2019 to 2020, with a 45% increase in deaths involving opioids, attributed to the increased presence of fentanyl in the county, state, and nation.

references.title: Opioid Overdose & Death in Orange County
references.abstract: This report summarizes opioid-related morbidity and mortality in Orange County, noting that from 2017 to 2021, the rate of death due to opioid overdose nearly tripled, from 7.9 deaths per 100,000 to 23.2, with the largest increase occurring in 2020, coinciding with the COVID-19 pandemic.

references.title: Two-Year Review of Fentanyl in Driving under the Influence and Postmortem Cases in Orange County, CA, USA
references.abstract: This study presents a two-year review of fentanyl concentrations in driving under the influence and postmortem cases in Orange County, CA, aiming to support the interpretation of fentanyl concentrations in postmortem cases and aid in the determination of the cause of death.

references.title: Sociodemographic and geographic disparities in excess fatal drug overdoses during the COVID-19 pandemic in California: A population-based study
references.abstract: This study estimates excess fatal drug overdoses in California during the COVID-19 pandemic, finding a 44% increase over the same period one year prior, with excess deaths driven by opioids, especially synthetic opioids, and disproportionately affecting non-Hispanic Black and Other non-Hispanic populations.

references.title: Opioid crisis: Big increase in CA fentanyl overdoses
references.abstract: This article discusses the exponential rise in fentanyl-related deaths in California since 2019, noting that between September 2021 and September 2022, 5,942 people died after ingesting the synthetic opioid, accounting for about 86% of all opioid-related deaths in the state.

references.title: Climate change and the capacity for water storage in California
references.abstract: This study discusses the impacts of climate variability on California's ability to store water effectively, particularly in relation to atmospheric river events and agricultural demands.

references.title: Evaluating the water storage capacity and demand in California's agricultural sector
references.abstract: This paper evaluates the limitations of California's water storage infrastructure in the context of increased precipitation events and agricultural water needs, highlighting critical gaps.

references.title: Assessing California's drought resilience: The role of water storage infrastructure
references.abstract: This research assesses the resilience of California’s water storage systems under shifting climate patterns, particularly focusing on atmospheric rivers and their implications for farmers.
authors: Angela J. S. P. L. Lee

references.title: The impact of atmospheric rivers on California's irrigation demands
references.abstract: This study examines how atmospheric river-related precipitation influences irrigation requirements for California's agricultural sector and the challenges posed by insufficient storage.

references.title: The challenges of maximizing water storage capacity amid climate change in California
references.abstract: This article discusses the challenges to maximizing water storage capacity in the face of climate change, focusing on atmospheric rivers and implications for agricultural stakeholders.
references.venue: Journal of Water Resources Planning and Management

references.title: Newsom Misled the Public About Wildfire Prevention Efforts Ahead of Worst Fire Season on Record
references.abstract: An analysis reveals that Governor Newsom's administration overstated the acreage treated under priority wildfire prevention projects, claiming 90,000 acres when only 11,399 acres were actually treated, raising concerns about transparency and effectiveness in wildfire mitigation efforts.
